examples.title: Algorithm: Two-Phase Warp Reduction for Trapezoidal Rule
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating the two-phase reduction strategy: first reduce within warps using shuffle instructions, then combine warp results using shared memory with a single synchronization barrier
examples.code:
ALGORITHM TwoPhaseWarpReduction
INPUT: a (lower bound), h (step size), n (number of intervals), blockDim (threads per block)
OUTPUT: Sum of f(x) using trapezoidal rule

PHASE 1: Thread-Level Computation
-----------------------------------
BEGIN
 tid ← threadIdx.x
 i ← threadIdx.x + blockIdx.x * blockDim.x
 
 // Each thread computes its portion of the integral
 IF i <= n THEN
 x_i ← a + i * h
 mySum ← f(x_i)
 // Handle endpoints (multiply by 0.5)
 IF i == 0 OR i == n THEN
 mySum ← mySum * 0.5
 END IF
 ELSE
 mySum ← 0.0
 END IF
END

PHASE 2: Intra-Warp Reduction (No Synchronization Needed)
----------------------------------------------------------
BEGIN
 // All threads in a warp execute in lockstep
 // Thread lane = tid % 32 (position within warp)
 
 // Warp-level reduction using shuffle-down
 val ← mySum
 val ← val + SHFL_DOWN(val, 16) // Thread lane i gets from lane i+16
 val ← val + SHFL_DOWN(val, 8) // Thread lane i gets from lane i+8
 val ← val + SHFL_DOWN(val, 4) // Thread lane i gets from lane i+4
 val ← val + SHFL_DOWN(val, 2) // Thread lane i gets from lane i+2
 val ← val + SHFL_DOWN(val, 1) // Thread lane i gets from lane i+1
 
 // After this: lane 0 of each warp holds the sum of that warp's values
END

PHASE 3: Inter-Warp Reduction (Single Synchronization)
-------------------------------------------------------
BEGIN
 // Each warp writes its result to shared memory
 IF tid % 32 == 0 THEN
 sdata[tid / 32] ← val // Store warp sum in dedicated slots
 END IF
 
 __syncthreads() // Only ONE synchronization needed!
 
 // First warp reduces all warp sums
 IF tid < blockDim.x / 32 THEN
 val ← sdata[tid]
 // Apply same warp reduction
 val ← val + SHFL_DOWN(val, 16)
 val ← val + SHFL_DOWN(val, 8)
 val ← val + SHFL_DOWN(val, 4)
 val ← val + SHFL_DOWN(val, 2)
 val ← val + SHFL_DOWN(val, 1)
 END IF
 
 // Write block result
 IF tid == 0 THEN
 partialSums[blockIdx.x] ← sdata[0]
 END IF
END

RESULT: partialSums array contains sum for each block, reduce on host to get final answer
examples.explanation: This pseudocode illustrates the key optimization: reducing synchronization barriers from log2(blockDim.x) to just 1. Phase 2 uses warp shuffle instructions which allow threads within the same warp (32 threads) to exchange register values without any synchronization because warp execution is lockstep. Phase 3 shows how only one __syncthreads() is needed to ensure all warp results are written to shared memory before the final reduction.

examples.title: CUDA: Volatile Warp Unrolling Approach
examples.description: C implementation using volatile keyword and manual warp unrolling to eliminate synchronization barriers within the last warp
examples.explanation: This implementation demonstrates the volatile warp unrolling approach. The key changes from naive tree reduction: (1) The for loop stops at s > 32 instead of s > 0, eliminating synchronization for the last warp; (2) The final 6 additions are manually unrolled; (3) A volatile pointer ensures the compiler doesn't optimize away shared memory accesses. For a 256-thread block, this reduces __syncthreads() calls from 8 to 3 (log2(256) - 5 = 8 - 5 = 3).
examples.timeComplexity: O(n/blockDim.x) per block, O(log2(blockDim.x) - 5) sync barriers vs O(log2(blockDim.x))
examples.spaceComplexity: O(blockDim.x) shared memory for full array, no extra registers for shuffle approach

examples.title: CUDA: Warp Shuffle Two-Phase Reduction
examples.description: C implementation using __shfl_down_sync warp shuffle instructions for efficient intra-warp reduction without shared memory
examples.explanation: This is the industry-standard warp shuffle approach. Key optimizations: (1) Intra-warp reduction uses __shfl_down_sync to exchange register values directly - no shared memory, no bank conflicts; (2) Only ONE __syncthreads() call is needed instead of log2(blockDim.x); (3) Shared memory requirement drops from blockSize to blockSize/32 (256 to 8 doubles for 256 threads). The warpReduceSum function performs 5 shuffle operations with deltas 16, 8, 4, 2, 1, which is the standard pattern for summing 32 values.

examples.title: CUDA: Comparing All Three Kernel Versions
examples.description: C implementation comparing performance characteristics of naive tree reduction, volatile approach, and warp shuffle approach
examples.explanation: This comprehensive comparison demonstrates all three approaches side-by-side. For a 256-thread block: V1 makes 8 __syncthreads() calls, V2 makes 3 (one per warp except the last), and V3 makes only 1. Shared memory usage drops from 2048 bytes (256 doubles) in V1/V2 to just 64 bytes (8 doubles) in V3. The timing results typically show V3 is fastest due to fewer synchronization barriers and reduced shared memory traffic. This is the pattern used in production libraries like NVIDIA's CUB.
